query: growth pattern of lymphoid tissues in children over time

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Issues of Concern -- Growth Velocity
retrieved_docs.content: The growth velocity is different at different stages of life. Also, different tissues grow at different rates at the same stage of life. The lymphoid tissues can exceed adult size at 6 years of age. Girls are taller than boys at 12 to 14 years, but later, they do not grow taller than their male counterparts. Growth velocity is maximum during infancy and adolescence. The head circumference reaches closer to adult size by 6 years of age. The prepubertal height velocity of less than 4 cm per year is of concern. During puberty, the height velocity is 10 to 12 cm per year in boys and 8 to 10 cm per year in girls. The prepubertal weight velocity of less than 1 kg per year is of concern. Weight velocity is highest during puberty, up to 8 kg per year.

retrieved_docs.title: Lymphoblastic Lymphoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The presentation of B- and T-cell LBL is different. T-LBL commonly involves the mediastinum (thymus); other possible sites include skin, tonsils, and spleen. Since T-LBL is more common than T-ALL, presentation with mediastinal masses with rapid growth is common. [1] T-lymphoblasts cannot be differentiated from B-lymphoblasts based on morphology, immunohistochemistry (IHC0, and flow cytometry are essential to render a diagnosis. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thymus -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Originally derived from the ventral 3rd pharyngeal pouch, the thymus enlarges from embryogenesis through approximately 3 years of age and then begins to regress during puberty. During development, the organ descends from the 3rd pharyngeal pouch into the superior mediastinum, settling posterior to the manubrium (see Image . The Thymus in a Full-Term Fetus). [8] The thymus is prominent in infants and young children, gradually coalescing and undergoing replacement by adipose tissue in early adulthood. Involution is thought to result from rising androgen levels in the circulation during puberty. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The thymus is functional from birth and is proportionally at its largest during infancy. [1] The thymus is responsible for expanding the T cell repertoire. Around puberty, the thymus begins to involute and decrease its output of mature T cells. [1] During the process of involution, there is epithelial atrophy and the replacement of lymphoid tissue with adipose tissue. [1] The thymus continues to develop T lymphocytes throughout adulthood, albeit at a much lower rate than in childhood.

retrieved_docs.title: Bruton Agammaglobulinemia (Archived) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Due to lack of B cell maturation, differnciation, and storage, lymphoid organs like the tonsils, spleen, adenoids, Peyer patches in the intestines, are poorly developed. The lymph nodes can, however, appear normal due to hypertrophy of T-cell areas.

retrieved_docs.title: Orbital Vascular Anomalies -- Etiology -- Characteristics Of Involuting Phase
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrosis and fat deposition on histology Low to absent thymidine labeling Rapid growth followed by regression

retrieved_docs.title: Follicular Lymphoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: FL typically presents with generalized painless lymphadenopathy, which is waxing and waning in nature. FL commonly involves axillary, cervical, femoral, and inguinal lymph nodes; rarely, it may present with an asymptomatic large mediastinal mass. Only 20% of patients with FL experience B symptoms (night sweats, fever, weight loss). Increased serum lactate  dehydrogenase (LDH) is also seen in 20% of the patients. Usually, FL only involves bone marrow and lymphoid organs. [8] [9] In children, FL majorly affects lymphoid tissues (tonsils and lymph nodes) of the head and neck. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Lymph Nodes -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Lymph nodes begin their development in utero as mesenchymal condensation, which later bulges to form a lymph sac. At the 13th gestational week, the T-cell region begins to develop, and by the 17th gestation week, the interdigitating reticulum cells (a subtype of T-cells) are found in the paracortical lymph node region, surrounded by lymphoid cells. B-cell regions within lymph nodes start their development at the 14th gestation week at the marginal sinus with a population of dendritic reticulum cell precursors, lymphoblasts, immunoblasts, and plasmablasts. By the 20th gestation week, incipient primary follicles are observable in the outer cortex containing lymphocytes. During the 12th and 14th gestation weeks, lymph nodes undergo granulopoiesis and erythropoiesis to produce undifferentiated blast cells, monocytes, and macrophages temporarily. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Inguinal Lymph Node -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The lymph node development starts from the 11 weeks of gestation as a mesenchymal condensation giving rise to the lymph node capsule and connective tissue. The T-cell lymphocytic region begins development during the 13 weeks. The B-cell lymphocytic region begins during the 14 weeks. From week 13 onward, monocytes and macrophages can be present with the lymph sac. During the 20 weeks, the follicle, paracortex, and medulla are discernable and continue to develop throughout gestation and after birth. [2] [3] The lymphatic system derives from the mesodermal embryological sheet.

retrieved_docs.title: Lymphadenopathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lymph node functions as an antigen filter for the body's reticuloendothelial system. It consists of a multi-layered sinus that sequentially exposes B-cell lymphocytes, T-cell lymphocytes, and macrophages to an afferent extracellular fluid. In this way, the immune system can recognize and react to foreign proteins and mount an immune response or sequester these proteins as appropriate. In this reaction, there is some multiplication of the responding resistant cell line; thus, the node increases in size. It is generally held that a node size is considered enlarged when it is more significant than 1 cm. However, the reality is that "normal" and "enlarged" criteria vary depending on the node's location and the patient's age. For example, children younger than 10 have more hypertrophic immune systems, and nodes up to 2 cm can be considered normal in some clinical situations. However, an epitrochlear node above 0.5 cm is deemed pathological in an adult.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Linked Immunodeficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The only characteristic physical examination finding of XLA is the absence of secondary lymphoid tissues rich in B cells, such as tonsils and adenoids. Also, palpable lymphoid tissue is not available in certain patients. However, peripheral nodes can be observed depending on the hypertrophy of the T-cell areas. Although it is diagnosed at an early stage due to the existence of a previously affected individual in the family, many patients are presented with recurrent infections. Diagnosis is established at the age of 2.6 in male children with family history, it is the age of 5.4 years in male children with no family history. Term neonates are under a passive control due to immunoglobulin transferred from mother in the last trimester and thus, the clinical symptoms in the patients may not be observed in up to 3 months. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palatine Tonsil (Faucial Tonsils) -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The development of the palatine tonsils begins during the fourteenth week of gestation. [6] Mononuclear wandering cells soon migrate into the mesenchyme. Crypts proliferate into the connective tissue and are infiltrated by T-lymphocytes at week 16. [6] The developing palatine tonsils are considered the first B-cell regions in fetal lymphoid tissue. [6] This series of developmental steps for the palatine tonsils arises from the 2nd pharyngeal pouch.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Weeks 6-8 -- Development -- Other Organs
retrieved_docs.content: Many other organs develop during weeks 6 through 8, including the pituitary gland, thymus, and adrenal cortex. At week 7, the embryo assumes a characteristic C-shape. At week 7, the ocular retina also begins to develop. The upper and lower limbs continue to grow. Also, facial structures such as the nostrils, eyelids, outer ears, lip, and palate develop, and at week 7, the head and face contours begin to emerge.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Craniofacial Growth, And Development -- Development -- Pharyngeal Pouches
retrieved_docs.content: The second pouch develops into palatine tonsillar crypts (although lymphatic tissue from the thymus and bone marrow enters much later in development). The third pouch bifurcates with the ventral wing becoming the thymus and the dorsal wing becoming the inferior parathyroid gland. The fourth pouch also bifurcates, the dorsal wing becoming the superior parathyroid gland and the ventral wing developing in the ultimobranchial body (this form the parafollicular cells of the thyroid). During development, rearrangement of these structures occurs, with the thymus descending down the neck towards the thorax and the inferior parathyroid gland moving below the superior one.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Lymphatic System -- Introduction -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Several organs in the body are considered to be lymphoid or lymphatic organs, given their role in the production of lymphocytes. These include the bone marrow, spleen, thymus, tonsils, lymph nodes, and other tissues. Lymphoid organs can be categorized as primary or secondary lymphoid organs. Primary lymphoid organs are those that produce lymphocytes, such as the bone marrow and thymus. Bone marrow is the primary site for the production of lymphocytes. The thymus is a glandular organ located anterior to the pericardium. It serves to mature and develop T cells, or thymus cell lymphocytes, in response to an inflammatory process or pathology. As individuals age, both their bone marrow and thymus reduce and accumulate fat. Secondary lymphoid organs serve as territories in which immune cells function and include the spleen, tonsils, lymph nodes, and various mucous membranes, such as in the intestines. The spleen is a purplish, fist-sized organ in the left upper abdominal quadrant that contributes to immune function by serving as a blood filter, storing lymphocytes within its white pulp, and being a site for an adaptive immune response to antigens. The lingual tonsils, palatine tonsils, and pharyngeal tonsils, or adenoids, work to prevent pathogens from entering the body. Mucous membranes in the gastrointestinal, respiratory, and genitourinary systems also function to prevent pathogens from entering the body.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thymus -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The thymus is located in the superior portion of the retrosternal mediastinum. Although bilobed, the organ exhibits considerable morphological variation among individuals and typically measures 30 to 40 mm in length and 25 to 35 mm in width. [3] The thymus contains 2 histological subcomponents, the cortex and the medulla, and is composed of epithelial, dendritic, mesenchymal, and endothelial cells. [4] [5] Unlike most other human organs, the thymus reaches full maturation in utero and undergoes progressive involution beginning around puberty. This process involves structural remodeling, during which organized parenchyma is gradually replaced by adipose tissue as immunologic activity declines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

The Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System is the associated anatomic site of ورم الغدد الليمفاوية, reflecting that these tumors arise in the tissues of the blood‑forming and lymphatic systems. Together, these relationships define lymph node cancer as a lymph gland tumor that belongs to the category of lymph node neoplasms and is localized to the hematopoietic and lymphatic system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are all examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. Each represents a malignant tumor arising in a specific anatomic site—thyroid cancer that has spread to cervical lymph nodes, a malignant growth originating in the nasal bone, and a malignant tumor arising in the thymus. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their aggressive, disease‑causing nature and underscores the need for thorough oncologic assessment and appropriate therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1130
retrieved_docs.content: Lymph node cancer (سرطان العقد اللمفاوية) is classified as a lymph gland tumor (ورم الغدد الليمفاوية), and this tumor type is situated within the broader category of malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified sites. Within the hierarchical structure, the neoplasm of lymph node (Neoplasm of lymph node) lists the lymph gland tumor as a child concept, indicating that the tumor is a specific form of lymph‑node neoplasm. The Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System is identified as the anatomic site associated with the lymph gland tumor, underscoring the relevance of this system in the disease’s location. The concept of spread to lymph nodes (انتشار إلى العقد اللمفاوية) functions as a parent category for malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified sites and is linked inversely to both the neoplasm of lymph node and lymph node cancer, reflecting that these neoplastic entities represent particular manifestations of disease dissemination to lymph nodes. Together, these relationships delineate how lymph node cancer, lymph gland tumor, and related neoplasms are organized within broader oncologic classifications and anatomical contexts, aiding clinical understanding of disease categorization and spread.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

Together, these concepts describe how the detection of a metastatic lesion serves as evidence of a malignant metastatic tumor involving the thymus, which is commonly associated with a palpable or radiographic thymic mass. Recognizing this association aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing metastatic disease affecting the thymus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 786
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical classification of neoplasms of the hematopoietic and immune systems includes the specific entity “ورم الغدد الليمفاوية” (lymph‑node tumor) as a narrower concept, and this tumor is also identified as a child of the broader category “Neoplasm of lymph node.” In the hierarchical structure, the clinical classification is linked to Hodgkin’s disease as its parent, indicating that Hodgkin’s disease represents a more general disease category within this classification framework. Anatomically, the lymph‑node tumor (“ورم الغدد الليمفاوية”) is associated with the Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System, reflecting its origin in the tissues of that system. Together, these relationships delineate how a specific lymph‑node neoplasm fits within broader disease classifications and its relevant anatomic context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled growth of cancerous cells. The thyroid‑originating tumor that spreads to cervical lymph nodes illustrates how a primary malignant neoplasm can metastasize, while the primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus exemplifies a malignant proliferation arising directly within the thymic tissue. Both conditions underscore the importance of recognizing malignant neoplastic proliferations as pathological processes that require accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted oncologic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1281
retrieved_docs.content: Lymph node cancer (سرطان العقد اللمفاوية) is classified as a lymph gland tumor (ورم الغدد الليمفاوية) and is considered a specific form of tumor that arises in the lymph nodes. This tumor type is included within the broader category of neoplasms of the lymph node, which have the lymph gland tumor as a child concept. The anatomical site most commonly associated with these tumors is the hematopoietic and lymphatic system, indicating that the disease originates in tissues responsible for blood formation and immune function. The process of spread to lymph nodes (انتشار إلى العقد اللمفاوية) is closely linked to lymph node neoplasms; it is regarded as a parent concept for neoplasms of the lymph node and is also a member of the group of malignant blood tumors (الأورام الدموية الخبيثة SMQ). Consequently, lymph node cancer can be understood both as a specific lymph gland tumor and as an instance of disease that involves dissemination to lymph nodes, reflecting its role within the spectrum of malignant hematopoietic and lymphatic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1039
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. As malignant tumors arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head and the thymus—each represents an uncontrolled growth of neoplastic cells that characterizes a serious pathological condition. Recognizing these neoplasms as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations emphasizes their role as aggressive cancerous processes that require prompt diagnostic evaluation and targeted oncologic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 892
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Hematopoietic and Immune Systems is a specific subclass that falls under the broader category of Metastatic Malignant Neoplasm to Head, with a particular focus on Metastatic Malignant Neoplasm to Lymph Nodes of Head. This classification is also directly linked as a child of Hodgkin’s disease, indicating that it pertains to Hodgkin’s disease presentations that involve metastatic spread to the head region, especially to the lymph nodes. Metastatic Malignant Neoplasm to Head represents a general group of cancers that have spread to structures within the head, while Metastatic Malignant Neoplasm to Lymph Nodes of Head is a more narrowly defined subset of this group, describing metastases specifically to the head’s lymphatic tissue. The hierarchical relationships among these entities illustrate how the clinical classification integrates both the anatomical site of metastasis (head lymph nodes) and the underlying disease entity (Hodgkin’s disease), providing a clear framework for diagnosis, classification, and management of hematopoietic and immune system neoplasms that present with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1236
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant neoplasm of other and unspecified sites is a broader classification that encompasses the condition referred to in Arabic as ورم الغدد الليمفاوية (lymph gland tumor). Within the hierarchy of lymphatic neoplasms, Neoplasm of lymph node is a parent entity that has ورم الغدد الليمفاوية as a child, indicating that the lymph gland tumor is a specific type of lymph node neoplasm. The Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System is identified as the anatomic site associated with ورم الغدد الليمفاوية, reflecting that this tumor arises in tissues of the blood‑forming and lymphatic systems. Together, these relationships clarify that the lymph gland tumor is a specific malignant neoplasm of the lymph nodes, situated within the broader category of malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified sites, and localized to the hematopoietic and lymphatic system.
